summary: I was born in Mon-ya, Laos in 1946 and lived there until the war forced me to flee. Laos was my home and the place where my husband, Pa Ying Vue, fought as a soldier in the Secret War. Even after the war, some of my family, like my two brothers, remained in Laos. It was a difficult place during those times, especially with the conflict and loss we experienced.

My husband, Pa Ying Vue, was a soldier who fought in the Secret War in Laos alongside General Vang Pao and the CIA. This war deeply affected my family, as it was the reason we had to flee Laos and cross the Mekong River into Thailand. During this time, my husband led troops and was eventually killed in action. The Secret War shaped much of my journey and the hardships I faced as a widow and refugee.

The Mekong River was a major obstacle for my family and me when we fled Laos around the late 1970s. I remember the night we crossed it at about 11 o'clock, using bamboo, plastic bags, and rubber tubes to keep us safe and hidden. Crossing the Mekong River was terrifying but necessary to reach Thailand and escape the war. It marked the beginning of our long journey as refugees.

After crossing the Mekong River, my family and I entered Thailand, where we walked through the jungle for five days without food or shelter. We lived hidden in the jungles of Thailand for about three years to stay safe. Later, around 1977, the Thai government placed us in the Nom Kai refugee camp. Thailand was both a refuge and a place of hardship for us during those years.

Pa Ying Vue was my husband and a soldier who fought bravely in the Secret War in Laos. He led a troop of 12 men supporting the CIA and General Vang Pao before he was killed in action during the war. We were married for about a year before having our first child, and together we had five children. His death was a turning point for me, as I had to lead my family through the jungle and into refugee camps alone.

Before fleeing Laos, my family and I traveled to the village of Plum Pu Na Thon to prepare for crossing the Mekong River. It was there that I gathered materials like bamboo and rubber tubes to help us safely cross the river at night. Plum Pu Na Thon was an important stop on our journey from Laos to Thailand during the war.

The Central Intelligence Agency, or CIA, was involved in the Secret War in Laos, working alongside General Vang Pao and soldiers like my husband, Pa Ying Vue. My husband led troops to assist the CIA American troops in fighting against Lao troops. The CIA's involvement was a key part of the conflict that forced my family to flee and eventually helped us gain access to the United States after my husband's death.

General Vang Pao was a leader during the Secret War in Laos, working with the CIA and soldiers like my husband. He was a significant figure in the fight against the Lao troops. My husband followed his leadership, and this connection played a role in our family's story and eventual resettlement in the United States after the war.

Around 1977, after living in the jungles of Thailand for three years, my family and I were placed in the Nom Kai refugee camp by the Thai government. The camp was crowded, with barbed wire fences, long food lines, and poor living conditions. We stayed there for about a year and a half while waiting for approval to immigrate to the United States. Life in the camp was difficult, but it was a step toward safety and a new life.

In 1979, my family and I were approved to resettle in the United States after being sponsored by a Hmong man named Mr. Thao. We first lived in Des Moines, Iowa, where the government helped us with basic needs. Later, we moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, and then to La Crosse, Wisconsin, where I spent most of my life. The United States has been a place of safety and opportunity for me and my children, and I am grateful for the life we have here.

I am part of the Hmong people, an ethnic group deeply affected by the Secret War in Laos. Our community fought alongside the CIA and General Vang Pao, and many, like my husband, sacrificed their lives. After the war, many Hmong families, including mine, became refugees and eventually resettled in the United States with the help of fellow Hmong sponsors like Mr. Thao. Our shared history has shaped my identity and journey.

When my family first arrived in the United States in 1979, we lived in Des Moines, Iowa for about two months. The government provided us with funds to cover rent, groceries, and other basic needs during that time. It was our first experience living in America, and though brief, it was an important transition from refugee life to a new beginning.

After living briefly in Iowa, my family moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, where we stayed for about three to four years. We had some relatives there, which made the transition easier for us. Tulsa was where my children grew up a bit more, and it was a significant part of our early life in the United States.

We moved to La Crosse, Wisconsin after Tulsa and lived there for about 10 to 12 years. Most of my life in the United States was spent in La Crosse, where my daughters got married and my sons remained bachelors for a time. It was a stable period for my family, and I consider it home for many years.

Now I live in Hudson, Wisconsin with my youngest son, Xai Vue, who is married with three children. I help watch my grandchildren here, which brings me joy. Hudson is where I have settled in my later years, surrounded by family and a peaceful environment.
